posts.name: Nick Gangas
posts.cooked: Does anyone know what the old domaine is doing now for closures since Laurent is gone ? specifically 2019.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: They haven’t changed.

posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked: so, still that white plastic thingy?

posts.name: Nick Gangas
posts.cooked: so just to be clear, Domaine Ponsot is using the same closure as when Laurent was running the show. If my memory is correct that is not the Diam. Correct ?

posts.cooked:
Laurent Ponsot

Technological closure - Laurent Ponsot

posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked:
if it’s like his white temp dots . . .
Also wonder if any other domaines use it.

posts.name: Phil T r o t t i e r
posts.cooked:
Domaine du Coulet from Matthieu Barret (Cornas) uses something similar:

TN: 2016 Domaine du Coulet Cornas Brise Cailloux + Weird Cork!

N.B: Super weird cork. This is my first time with this. On top of the plastic capsule, the cork looks synthetic but the end of the cork is hard plastic. See picture below. Anybody ever encountered those before?

I haven’t encountered it in Burgundies yet…

posts.name: Brian Buzzini
posts.cooked:
alan_weinberg:

if it’s like his white temp dots . . .
Also wonder if any other domaines use it.

I’ve seen them with Francois Carillon’s Burg Blanc.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: No, it’s Ardeaseal. And it’s only for 750s, magnums are natural cork I believe.

posts.name: Jacob vandergeest
posts.cooked:
Domaine Françoise André (Beaune) uses Ardeaseal. At least since vintage 2016.

IMG_20230530_174009~21920×2451 181 KB
Philippe Colin uses this closure.

posts.name: D Borczon
posts.cooked: I am just wary of anything made out of petroleum that is going to stay in contact with alcohol that I will consume…at least over a period of years. Couple of months, maybe. Diam still seems more sensible option.

posts.name: D Borczon
posts.cooked: Printing looks nice though…

posts.name: larry schaffer
posts.cooked:
Dennis_Borczon:

I am just wary of anything made out of petroleum that is going to stay in contact with alcohol that I will consume…at least over a period of years. Couple of months, maybe. Diam still seems more sensible option.

That said, have there been any ‘known problems’ with these closures? They have been used for awhile IIRC and I’ve never heard of anything leaching into the wines.

Cheers

posts.name: D Borczon
posts.cooked: Yes, I get it they have been tested. I read the stuff on the website. But I doubt they have 10 year data on the substance in question being immersed in 14% alcohol solution, and the effects on flavor. I know you can stand these bottles up, and I would do that if I were planning to age these wines. Guess if it were widely adopted then we’ll have to re design all of our wine storing racks? Sigh.

posts.name: larry schaffer
posts.cooked:
These have been used for quite some time, no? At least 5 years and possibly longer? Anyone else have info on that

Cheers

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
Dennis_Borczon:

I am just wary of anything made out of petroleum that is going to stay in contact with alcohol that I will consume…at least over a period of years. Couple of months, maybe. Diam still seems more sensible option.

“The closure is made of three parts: The chassis helps maintain structure, rigidity, and elasticity; the body gives the closure its shape and is what the corkscrew punctures through; and the shield, which is made of the same completely inert polymer found in pacemakers, is what dictates the oxygen transfer rate…" – Spirited, 2021

Dennis_Borczon:

Yes, I get it they have been tested. I read the stuff on the website. But I doubt they have 10 year data on the substance in question being immersed in 14% alcohol solution, and the effects on flavor. I know you can stand these bottles up, and I would do that if I were planning to age these wines. Guess if it were widely adopted then we’ll have to re design all of our wine storing racks? Sigh.

“TheArdeaSeal wine closure has been on the market for roughly 20 years. Originally founded as Guala Seal, in about 2015 it rebranded as the ArdeaSeal wine closure.” – same 2021 article

link_counts.title: Sealed Tight: Innovations in closure materials and design continue to delight. - Spirited Magazine
ARDEASEAL Multi component stoppers for quality wines, design, production and sale

posts.name: D Borczon
posts.cooked: if it is that awesome, why have more wineries not adopted it? is it price? resistance to change? Diam seems to have more acceptance.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked: It looks expensive and, let’s face it, weird. I think it may also require special bottling equipment, while Diam boasts that it can be used without modifications to machinery.

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
Dennis_Borczon:

But I doubt they have 10 year data on the substance in question being immersed in 14% alcohol solution, and the effects on flavor.

I don’t know about “them” but I have enough data from personal experience, including wines I’ve aged myself for close to 10 years - the wines themselves having been in contact with the closure for more than ten years. No noticeable oddities whatsoever. The wines have been just fine and in a terrific shape.

posts.name: D Borczon
posts.cooked: Interesting. More wineries should consider them. If the cost is favorable vs cork. Just hope these recyclable corks don’t all end up in landfills or at the bottom of the ocean. At least cork is still the ultimate biodegradable. Suppose it has that still going for it!

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: They have used them since 2008, so they do have ten year data.

posts.name: p. raghib
posts.cooked:
This is correct, 750s under ardeaseal, mags&larger under natural cork. I’ve never understood why they didn’t do everything under ardeseal and I was told at the domaine they didn’t make them/offer them which struck me as really odd.

The benefit of this is mags are usually orphans at auctions, I know they are not board darlings-but the wines can be very good. I don’t buy the 750s, but I do buy the mags.

participants.name: Phil T r o t t i e r
